# RFC-0002: Workspace, Stack, And Local Placement Contract

> **Version:** 0.7.0 | **Status:** normative | **Phase:** test

---

## 1. Summary

### [RFC-0002:C-SUMMARY] Summary (Informative) <a id="rfc-0002c-summary"></a>

This RFC governs the workspace, serving stacks, and machine-local execution bindings: what one workspace revision fixes and excludes, how stacks own framework integration and Pixi realization facts, how local placements bind portable servers to concrete machines and ranks, dirty-workspace execution policy, the committed Pixi lifecycle from clean lock production to content-confirmed reuse, stack realization checks, ad-hoc execution, and per-process runtime JIT cache allocation. [RFC-0001](../rfc/RFC-0001.md) holds the specification overview and authority map.

*Since: v0.1.0*

---

## 2. Specification

### [RFC-0002:C-WORKSPACE-AUTHORITY] Workspace And Local Authority (Normative) <a id="rfc-0002c-workspace-authority"></a>

A shareable execution baseline MUST be identified by one workspace revision that fixes the relevant source tree and submodule revisions, Pixi manifest and lock, framework integration package selection, reusable definitions, and recipes.

Workspace schema version 2 MUST expose `models`, `stacks`, `servers`, `evals`, `benches`, `workload_suites`, `recipes`, `images`, and `external_images` as its reusable definition classes. It MUST NOT expose environment, source-set, or serve-profile definition classes.

A shared model definition MUST provide logical model identity and `served_name` but MUST NOT contain a model-weight locator.

A stack MUST be the single workspace authority for `integration`, `pixi_environment`, ordered workspace-relative `source_paths`, `checks`, and `image_postprocess`. A stack MAY declare no source paths.

A workspace loader MUST reject an unsupported schema version, including schema version 1, before interpreting definitions. It MUST NOT partially translate or merge definitions from an unsupported schema into schema version 2.

Ordinary execution of a named server or recipe MUST NOT require the operator to restate a model, stack, server behavior, measurement definition, framework integration version, or other reusable fact already selected by the workspace definition.

The public CLI MUST accept global `--workspace <dir>` before the command to select the workspace root explicitly; omission uses the standard workspace discovery path.

A workspace MAY organize reusable definitions across multiple files. File organization MUST NOT create implicit behavior-affecting precedence between equivalent definitions.

Multi-file organization MUST compose the root `.inferlab/workspace.toml` with fragments under `.inferlab/workspace.d/*.toml` as a disjoint union of identifier-keyed definitions.

An identifier declared by two workspace files MUST be rejected at load with an error naming both files.

`schema_version` and any workspace-global scalar MUST appear only in the root workspace file; a fragment that declares one MUST be rejected at load naming the fragment.

Every path component of the workspace configuration files — the `.inferlab` directory, the root workspace file, the `workspace.d` directory, and every workspace fragment — MUST be a non-symbolic-link filesystem entry; a symbolic-link component MUST be rejected at load, because the workspace source digest records link text rather than target content.

Every component of each declared stack source path below the workspace root MUST likewise be a non-symbolic-link entry, rejected at load for the same reason.

Every symbolic link effectively present in the digested worktree — below the workspace root, outside every `.git` entry, outside the workspace source exclusions (the local-bindings file and the `.inferlab` `cache`, `records`, `runtime`, and `scratchpads` subtrees), and outside git-ignored directories, which the walk does not enter — MUST resolve within the workspace root and outside every `.git` entry. A digest-visible link — one the source digest records: tracked, or untracked and not git-ignored, including a link replacing a tracked entry — MUST further carry a relative target that lexically resolves to identity-covered content: not under a workspace source exclusion and not content git-ignored as judged by the target's owning repository. A target that lexically resolves onto or through another digest-visible link is judged against its link-resolved destination, substituting digest-visible link text until no digest-visible link component remains; a digest-visible target that resolves onto or through a machine-local link MUST be rejected naming the starting link, its target, and the machine-local link, because the machine-local link's text is outside the recorded identity and its retarget would change effective content under an unchanged digest; a substitution chain that revisits a link is a cycle and MUST be rejected naming the starting link and its target. A violating link MUST be rejected when the workspace snapshot claims source identity, naming the link path and target — and the declaring stack when the link lies under one of its source paths — because the source digest records link text rather than target content.

A git-ignored link is machine-local state no identity claim covers, so containment alone binds it, and an absolute target is judged by whether it resolves under this workspace's root. A git-ignored directory is a machine-local tree outside the digested worktree: the walk neither enters it nor judges its contents, because the digest cannot see them and a digest-visible link targeting git-ignored content is already rejected. A digest-visible link whose target is identity-covered remains permitted, as is a dangling internal target. Tracking state otherwise affects dirtiness, not containment; resolution is lexical for relative targets, and every intermediate link is enumerated and judged on its own because the walk covers the whole digested worktree rather than only declared stack source paths.

Local bindings MUST remain separate from shareable workspace content.

Inferlab MUST discover local bindings from `.inferlab/local.toml` under the workspace root by default.

A workflow that permits local-binding selection MUST allow an operator to select an alternate local-bindings file explicitly with `--local <file>`.

Local bindings MAY supply a default placement, model-weight locators, machines, devices, ordered port pools, launch access, credentials, builders, caches, artifact locations, container facts, and other machine-private facts.

Local bindings MUST NOT contain persistent overrides for behavior-affecting model, stack, server, case, recipe, Eval, Bench, workload-suite, image, or external-image settings.

Long-running server and closed-loop recipe workflows MUST permit execution from a dirty workspace for local development.

Dirty-workspace resolution MUST identify the baseline revision, the effective dirty source state by content digest, and that revision-only reproduction is not possible.

The workspace serving closure MUST NOT include lm-eval, AIPerf, an Inferlab Eval or Bench runner, or an Eval-only or Bench-only Pixi environment.

The Inferlab-owned measurement toolchains ([RFC-0004:C-INFERLAB-TOOLCHAIN](../rfc/RFC-0004.md#rfc-0004c-inferlab-toolchain)) MUST be fixed by the installed Inferlab release and remain outside workspace package authority; workspace-side integration packages remain inside the workspace dependency authority ([RFC-0006:C-INTEGRATIONS](../rfc/RFC-0006.md#rfc-0006c-integrations)).

**Rationale:** A stack gives each framework closure one shareable owner without making machine placement or model-weight paths portable configuration. Git and Pixi provide the serving baseline shared by collaborators, while a conventional local file supplies private machine facts. Explicit schema rejection is safer than translating a removed ownership model into superficially similar fields. The containment walk covers the digested worktree rather than only declared source paths because a link outside those paths still enters source identity and can bridge into framework-consumed content. Git-ignored directories stay outside that identity because real editable installations legitimately place machine-local links there.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0002:C-PIXI-ENVIRONMENT-LIFECYCLE] Pixi Stack Environment Lifecycle (Normative) <a id="rfc-0002c-pixi-environment-lifecycle"></a>

A stack's local serving realization MUST be defined by the committed Pixi manifest and lock together with the Pixi environment selected by that stack.

The schema-version-2 CLI MUST NOT expose `env lock` or `env status`; `workspace lock` and `stack status` replace them.

The public CLI MUST provide `workspace lock`, which can produce the full workspace lock from a clean local package prefix.

`workspace lock` MUST NOT load local bindings or accept `--local`. It MUST NOT require manual intermediate manifest edits or out-of-band package installation. Successful lock production MUST preserve the authoritative workspace manifest byte-for-byte and leave the resulting full lock as the workspace lock. After a handled failure or interruption, it MUST restore the authoritative manifest and any pre-existing workspace lock.

Intermediate package state used while producing a lock MUST NOT become a second manifest, lock, stack identity, or experiment input.

Ordinary workspace setup MUST install the selected stack environments from the committed lock without updating that lock or the authoritative manifest.

Dry-run, long-running server, and closed-loop recipe workflows MUST activate the already installed Pixi environment selected by the server's stack without installing packages or updating the manifest or lock, unless a runtime image substitutes for the local stack realization ([RFC-0003:C-RUNTIME-WORKFLOWS](../rfc/RFC-0003.md#rfc-0003c-runtime-workflows)). In that case the image is the serving realization and no local serving installation is consulted.

When no image substitutes, those workflows MUST fail before server launch or measurement execution — naming the stack, Pixi environment, and required locked installation action — when the selected environment has never been installed, when the committed lock is not up to date with the manifest, or when the installation has not been confirmed usable against the current manifest and lock content.

An installation is confirmed usable against manifest and lock content only by a workflow within this specification having observed it usable against that exact content. A workspace revision change that leaves both contents unchanged MUST NOT invalidate an existing confirmation. When either content changes, the operator MUST reinstall every affected stack environment and cause a workflow within this specification to reconfirm it before use.

The public CLI MUST provide `stack status [STACK]`. With a stack identifier it MUST report whether that stack's selected Pixi environment is confirmed usable against the current manifest and lock; without one it MUST report that state for every declared stack. `stack status` MUST NOT load local bindings, accept `--local`, install packages, or update the manifest or lock.

Before launching any server process, long-running server and closed-loop recipe workflows MUST execute the selected stack's declared checks against each distinct machine realization the launch consumes — the controller's installation and each remote machine's ([RFC-0002:C-ENVIRONMENT-CHECKS](../rfc/RFC-0002.md#rfc-0002c-environment-checks)). A failed check MUST fail before any process launches and present the declared repair hint. This preflight does not apply when a built image substitutes, because the image realization was checked during assembly, or when a declared external image substitutes, because the workspace makes no realization-check qualification claim for it.

Experiment execution MUST NOT create a server-specific, recipe-specific, case-specific, or source-path-specific Pixi manifest, lock, or materialized environment closure.

Dry-run output and execution records MUST identify the selected stack, its Pixi environment, and the committed manifest and lock digests.

The stack environment lifecycle is independent of the Inferlab-owned measurement-toolchain lifecycle in [RFC-0004:C-INFERLAB-TOOLCHAIN](../rfc/RFC-0004.md#rfc-0004c-inferlab-toolchain).

**Rationale:** A committed lock gives collaborators one fixed workspace installation model, while the stack identifies which locked environment realizes a framework closure. Separating lock production, locked installation, and no-sync execution keeps package mutation out of experiments. Content confirmation catches a stale installed prefix even when the workspace revision changes independently, and `stack status` exposes that question without reviving an environment definition class.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0002:C-ENVIRONMENT-CHECKS] Stack Realization Checks (Normative) <a id="rfc-0002c-environment-checks"></a>

A stack MAY declare realization checks and image-realization postprocess steps. Both are committed workspace content: the stack owns what its framework realization must satisfy, while Inferlab owns the execution contract.

The committed Pixi lock is the single authority for package content, but each installed instance of the stack's selected Pixi environment — the local workspace environment, each remote machine's environment, and an image environment — is a distinct realization produced by installer tooling whose behavior the lock does not fully determine. Checks assert stack-declared invariants against a realization.

A check MUST be a workspace-relative Python script executed with the selected Pixi environment's Python interpreter. Exit status zero is the sole pass signal. A check MUST NOT mutate the realization it examines. Check output MUST report facts, not remedies; a check declaration MAY carry a repair hint for the operator.

One stack check set MUST serve every realization point. This specification requires execution at image-build entry against the local stack realization and inside image assembly against the image realization ([RFC-0007:C-IMAGE-BUILD](../rfc/RFC-0007.md#rfc-0007c-image-build)), and before server launch against each distinct machine realization the launch consumes ([RFC-0002:C-PIXI-ENVIRONMENT-LIFECYCLE](../rfc/RFC-0002.md#rfc-0002c-pixi-environment-lifecycle)).

A failed check MUST fail the consuming workflow; there is no warning level. Repair responsibility follows realization ownership. The operator owns workspace realizations: a check failure there means the realization drifted, the failure MUST present the declared repair hint, and Inferlab MUST NOT mutate the environment itself. The build owns an image realization: a check failure there means a systematic defect in build inputs or procedure, the failure MUST NOT present the repair hint, and the remedy is correcting the inputs and rebuilding.

An image-realization postprocess step MUST be a workspace-relative Python script executed only during image assembly, after environment installation and before the checks, as a deterministic finishing step of that realization. Its effect MUST derive only from the frozen build context and the environment's locked closure. Postprocess steps MUST NOT execute against a workspace realization.

Every executed check MUST be recorded with its stack and realization — including machine when machine-scoped — its outcome, and captured output in the consuming workflow record. An infrastructural check-execution failure MUST still finalize that record.

**Rationale:** Realization drift is realization-specific and cheapest to catch before device-backed work. Keeping checks with the stack gives the invariant one owner across local, remote, and image realizations, while repair remains with the owner of the mutable host or immutable build process.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0002:C-RUNTIME-CACHE] Runtime JIT Cache Allocation (Normative) <a id="rfc-0002c-runtime-cache"></a>

Before final process rendering, Inferlab MUST allocate one runtime JIT cache root for every concrete server process allocation.

A local machine binding MAY provide the storage root from which that process cache is allocated. When it does not, Inferlab MUST use deterministic workspace-local state.

A runtime JIT cache namespace MUST include the resolved workspace source state, selected stack and Pixi environment identity, concrete machine identity, and process identity. When a built or external image substitutes for the local stack realization, the namespace MUST key on the image's immutable identity in place of the invoking workspace source state and Pixi environment identity.

Processes assigned to different machines or different process identities MUST NOT receive the same mutable cache path, even when their storage roots refer to one shared filesystem.

The selected framework integration MUST receive the allocated process cache root with each concrete allocation during final rendering.

Framework-specific cache families, subdirectories, and environment-variable names MUST remain owned by the selected integration rather than the framework-neutral control plane.

An explicit framework environment override MAY replace an integration-provided cache environment default, and the effective value MUST remain visible in dry-run output and execution records.

Real execution MUST create the allocated cache root on the target machine before materializing launch files or spawning the process.

Dry-run output and execution records MUST preserve each process cache storage root, namespace inputs, allocated path, and effective framework cache environment values.

**Rationale:** Runtime-generated compiler and kernel state is necessary but unsafe under an implicit shared home directory. Per-process allocation isolates mutable state, and image-keyed namespaces ensure the software identity that generates cache entries is the identity that names them. The integration owns framework variable mapping while Rust owns allocation and evidence.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0002:C-ADHOC-EXECUTION] Ad-Hoc Stack And Image Execution (Normative) <a id="rfc-0002c-adhoc-execution"></a>

The public CLI MUST provide `run`, which executes one operator-supplied command inside a selected stack or image realization, attaches it to the operator's standard streams, and exits with that command's exit status.

Realization selection MUST offer the local realization of a named stack, the host-platform assembly of a selected image build record, or a declared external image. These selections MUST be mutually exclusive.

The schema-version-2 CLI MUST NOT accept `run --environment`; `run --stack` replaces it.

For local execution, `--stack <stack>` MUST select the stack. When the workspace declares exactly one stack, omission MUST select it; when it declares more than one, omission MUST fail requiring an explicit stack.

When an image build record is selected, the executed realization MUST be the stack realization captured by that record. Explicit stack selection combined with either image selection MUST be rejected.

Local execution MUST activate the already installed selected Pixi environment under [RFC-0002:C-PIXI-ENVIRONMENT-LIFECYCLE](../rfc/RFC-0002.md#rfc-0002c-pixi-environment-lifecycle), installing no packages and updating neither manifest nor lock. It MUST fail before executing the command when the environment is absent or the lock is stale. Because `run` produces no qualification evidence, it MUST NOT require or create content-confirmation state.

The operator command MUST execute as an executable. A command name that collides with a package-manager task MUST NOT resolve to that task.

Built-image execution MUST use the image's activation entrypoint ([RFC-0007:C-IMAGE-BUILD](../rfc/RFC-0007.md#rfc-0007c-image-build)). External-image execution MUST use an explicit command override and assume nothing about the image's entrypoint or activation.

`run` MUST NOT execute stack realization checks, allocate workflow runtime caches, create an execution record, or satisfy a qualification obligation. It MUST NOT accept an alternate local-bindings selector.

Image execution MUST apply no implicit host mount. It MUST accept explicit operator-declared host mounts and device selections; either kind combined with local stack execution MUST be rejected. Every mount source MUST be an existing absolute host path bound at the identical container path, read-only unless explicitly marked writable. An unrepresentable mount MUST fail rather than be altered.

Without an explicit device selection, image execution MUST request no devices.

The shipped operator skill MUST direct agents to run ad-hoc environment commands through this operation ([RFC-0008:C-AGENT-PLUGIN](../rfc/RFC-0008.md#rfc-0008c-agent-plugin)) and prohibit direct invocation of binaries from a materialized Pixi prefix.

**Rationale:** Directly invoking a binary from a materialized prefix loses the activation contract. One sanctioned command keeps probes aligned with product execution while deliberately producing no qualification evidence. Stack selection replaces a removed environment definition, and explicit image mounts and devices avoid inventing workflow allocation facts for an ad-hoc command.

*Since: v0.4.0*

### [RFC-0002:C-LOCAL-PLACEMENT] Machine-Local Placement And Model Resolution (Normative) <a id="rfc-0002c-local-placement"></a>

Local bindings MUST define machine-private execution facts independently from workspace servers and cases.

A machine binding MUST be able to declare `host`, ordered `devices`, one ordered `ports` pool, and its `launch`, `workspace`, `cache`, and `container` facts. The ordered port pool replaces any distinction between a primary port and extra ports.

A local placement MUST NOT reference a server or server case. Compatibility between a selected placement and the resolved server MUST be decided during resolution.

A placement MUST use exactly one of two allocation forms: ordered machine pools through `machines` and optional `roles.<role>.machines`, or explicit role-local bindings through `roles.<role>`. A machine-pool placement MAY declare one global pool and role-specific pools that replace the global pool for those roles.

An explicit role-local binding MUST use exactly one canonical shape. A direct shape MUST declare one `machine`, ordered `devices`, and an optional `endpoint_port`; it represents replica zero and rank zero. A single multi-rank shape MUST declare only a `ranks` list of at least two rank bindings; it represents replica zero. A multiple-replica shape MUST declare only a `replicas` list of at least two replica entries. Every entry in a multiple-replica list MUST itself be either a direct shape or a multi-rank shape. Fields from different shapes MUST NOT coexist.

The position of an entry in `roles.<role>.replicas` MUST determine its replica index. The position of each rank binding in a `ranks` list MUST determine its rank index. A rank binding MUST declare one `machine`, ordered `devices`, and an optional `endpoint_port`. Local bindings MUST NOT require or accept authored replica or rank indices.

The control plane MUST consume the integration-planned whole-replica device count when applying a machine pool. It MUST place an entire model-serving replica on one machine as one rank. It MUST NOT silently split a replica across machines; when no eligible machine can hold the entire replica, resolution MUST require explicit replica bindings.

Every explicit model-serving rank MUST bind at least one device. The rank device counts within one explicit replica MUST collectively equal that replica's planned device count. Explicit replica bindings MAY place one replica across multiple machines and MAY give different replicas or roles different rank shapes.

Every concrete device allocated to concurrently launched ranks on one machine MUST be declared by that machine and MUST NOT overlap another allocation. A rank's explicit endpoint port, and every port allocated from a machine pool, MUST belong to the selected machine's declared ordered port pool and MUST NOT overlap another live allocation.

A machine-pool allocation MUST be deterministic from declared machine and device order. It MUST bind a router to the first eligible machine in `roles.router.machines` or the global `machines` pool with no devices. An explicit-binding placement used by a routed server MUST declare `roles.router` in the direct shape.

The canonical router role's explicit binding MUST resolve to replica zero and rank zero, and its device list MUST be empty. The router remains a concrete zero-device process allocation with a machine and ports.

A placement selector MUST resolve in this order: an explicit invocation `--placement`, the local bindings' optional `default_placement`, or the sole declared placement. More than one remaining placement is ambiguous and MUST fail naming the candidates. A workflow that needs placement and resolves none MUST fail before integration rendering or external effect.

A local `model_weights` binding keyed by shared model identifier MAY declare fallback `locator`, `machine_locators`, or both. For each model-serving rank, resolution MUST select the locator for that rank's machine when present, otherwise the fallback locator. A missing locator for any model-serving rank MUST fail resolution naming the model, machine, role, replica, and rank. A router rank MUST receive no model locator. A map containing only machine-specific locators is valid.

The resolved placement MUST preserve the selected placement identity, selection source, every derived role/replica/rank coordinate, machine, devices, endpoint and auxiliary ports, launch facts, cache facts, and each model locator with its machine-specific or fallback source.

**Rationale:** Portable server intent does not know where collaborators store weights or how their machines expose devices and ports. Whole-replica pool allocation keeps the convenient case deterministic. Role-local explicit bindings leave ordinary one-rank replicas flat, while `ranks` and `replicas` expose only the nesting demanded by multi-rank or multi-replica topologies. Deriving replica and rank indices from list positions removes duplicate numbering authority and makes gaps, duplicates, and disagreement between indices and list order unrepresentable. Per-rank locator resolution remains necessary because machines participating in one replica may mount the same weights at different paths.

*Since: v0.6.0*

### [RFC-0002:C-WORKSPACE-DISCOVERY] Public Workspace Discovery (Normative) <a id="rfc-0002c-workspace-discovery"></a>

The public CLI MUST provide `workspace show` with an optional `--json` output mode.

`workspace show` MUST load and validate only the shareable workspace configuration. It MUST NOT load local bindings, invoke an integration, inspect a stack realization, or perform any external action.

Human-readable output MUST identify the available stacks, models, servers, server topology and cases, default case selection, recipes and their selected servers and workload suites, and the Eval, Bench, and workload-suite catalog.

JSON output MUST emit one machine-readable canonical view of the merged public workspace definition, including its schema version and identifier-keyed definition maps. Diagnostics MUST remain on stderr.

**Rationale:** Authors need a fast configuration and discovery path before they have machine-local placement or a framework environment. Returning the validated public authority keeps discovery distinct from dry-run, which remains the exact effective execution plan.

*Since: v0.7.0*
